title: Delete a task

story.i_want_to: delete a task by its ID
story.so_that: I can remove tasks that are no longer relevant and keep my list clean

rationale: Not all tasks end up being done — some become irrelevant or were added by mistake. Without delete, the task list accumulates noise over time, making it harder to focus on what matters.

acceptance_criteria.description: Running `todo delete 3` removes the task with id=3 from tasks.json.
Before deleting, the app prints 'Delete task {id} [{status}]: {description}? [y/N] ' and waits for confirmation. The status (pending/done) is shown so the user can catch mistakes.
If the user enters 'y' or 'Y', the task is deleted and the app prints 'Deleted task {id}: {description}' to stdout.
If the user enters anything other than y/Y (including just pressing Enter), the app prints 'Cancelled.' to stdout and exits with code 0 without modifying the task list.
Running `todo delete --force 3` or `todo delete -f 3` skips the confirmation prompt and deletes immediately. This supports scripting use cases.
If the task ID does not exist, the app prints 'Error: Task {id} not found.' to stderr and exits with code 1.
Running `todo delete` without an ID prints 'Error: Please provide a task ID. Usage: todo delete <id>' to stderr and exits with code 1.
Other tasks and their IDs are not affected by the deletion (IDs are stable, not re-assigned).
If the provided ID is not a positive integer, the app prints 'Error: Task ID must be a positive number.' to stderr and exits with code 1.

test_cases.name: Delete existing task with confirmation
preconditions: tmp_dir with tasks.json containing tasks id=1 and id=2
expected_results: Returns deleted task for confirmation message

test_cases.name: Delete non-existent task returns error
preconditions: tmp_dir with tasks.json containing task id=1
expected_results: Raises ValueError or returns error indicating task not found

test_cases.name: Delete does not re-assign IDs of remaining tasks
preconditions: tmp_dir with tasks.json containing tasks id=1, id=2, id=3
expected_results: Remaining tasks have id=1 and id=3 (unchanged)

test_cases.name: CLI delete command with confirmation (user enters y)
preconditions: tmp_dir with tasks.json containing task id=1 'Buy groceries' status='pending'
steps: Run `python todo.py delete 1` with HOME=tmp_dir, pipe 'y' to stdin

test_cases.name: CLI delete command cancelled (user enters N)
preconditions: tmp_dir with tasks.json containing task id=1 'Buy groceries'
steps: Run `python todo.py delete 1` with HOME=tmp_dir, pipe 'n' to stdin

test_cases.name: CLI delete --force skips confirmation
preconditions: tmp_dir with tasks.json containing task id=1 'Buy groceries'
steps: Run `python todo.py delete --force 1` with HOME=tmp_dir (no stdin)

test_cases.name: Non-numeric ID shows error
steps: Run `python todo.py delete abc` with HOME=tmp_dir
expected_results: stderr contains 'Error: Task ID must be a positive number.'

test_cases.name: Delete last task then list shows empty state
preconditions: tmp_dir with tasks.json containing only task id=1
expected_results: First command succeeds with exit code 0
Second command prints 'No tasks yet. Add one with: todo add "your task"'

technical_notes: Use list comprehension to filter out the task: [t for t in tasks if t['id'] != task_id]
Compare list length before/after to detect if task was actually found
IDs are never re-used or re-assigned — this is important for consistency
Parse --force/-f flag from sys.argv before extracting the ID
Use input() for the confirmation prompt; handle EOFError for piped empty stdin (treat as 'no')
Show task status in confirmation prompt so user can catch mistakes
All error messages to stderr, all success messages to stdout
